required_complexity_patterns.notes: Yes/no question as paired button elements, not native radio inputs
At least two fields with aria-label only and no visible label element
Label text split across multiple spans with no space between words
Combobox: button or input with aria-haspopup=listbox plus listbox/options
Newsletter or login decoy form separate from job application form

prompt_summary: Create a messy, production-real ashby-style job application (single-page, large, tier=high). Widgets: combobox. Required: Yes/no question as paired button elements, not native radio inputs Required: At least two fields with aria-label only and no visible label element Required: Label text split across multiple spans with no space between words Required: Combobox: button or input with aria-haspopup=listbox plus listbox/options Required: Newsletter or login decoy form separate from job application form Reference DOM (CSS hidden wizard steps): Multi-step wizard with only one step visible via CSS class toggling Reference DOM (Triple nested fieldsets): Fieldset inside fieldset inside fieldset with legends at each level Reference DOM (Output with range): Years of experience via range input with output element display Do NOT produce a tutorial-style form where every field is label[for]+native input. Mix native controls with non-semantic production DOM from the patterns below. Implement ALL required complexity patterns: pill-yes-no, aria-label-only, glued-label-text, custom-combobox, decoy-form. Include at least one custom widget (div role=textbox, pill buttons, or listbox combobox). Include at least one labeling strategy besides label-for (aria-label, aria-labelledby, glued spans, placeholder-only). Layout: fieldset stacks with legend-only section titles. Avoid generic centered SaaS card as the only structure. High tier: include shadow DOM host OR iframe apply shell OR wizard/conditional reveal with hidden fields. Mirror structural messiness like these references: CSS hidden wizard steps; Triple nested fieldsets; Output with range.
